we weren't comfortable with before.

To answer that question,
we started by asking our teams

After conducting multiple interviews and surveys

project managers, team leads,

a clear set of challenges emerge
on how we manage project data.
Today, project data is not always quickly

Teams often struggle to find the most current version
of information,

Teams schedules often become misaligned,

Even though we have quality control processes in place,

Ultimately, what we found is our standards and processes
that need to be simpler,
more visible, and more automated
so they are consistently followed.
On most projects,
we already perform major quality checks

These milestones are natural opportunities
to verify that the project gets on track,
and that the right data is being collected and reported.
From the initial pursuit phase,
all the way through final handover.

Most of the information we needed
with an Autodesk Construction Cloud,

at a very granular level.
And dynamic project environment,

It also made it harder for our teams

With the emergence of MCP servers,
we finally had an easier way to make that possible.
By combining AI with MCP servers,

analyze it and use it to perform automated quality checks
throughout the lifecycle of a project.
When we applied those requirements,
this is the general architecture we developed.
We divided the implementation into two phases,
one before DevCon and one after DevCon.
Phase one is our proof of concept stage,
running in a sandbox environment.
In this stage, we started our development
using Cloud Desktop
because it made integration and testing

The MCP server itself was hosted on a local machine

At this stage, the automation was triggered manually.
A user would initiate the process with the prompt and cloud,

Phase two moves this system

Instead of manually triggering the workflow,

Also in Phase two, since our firm is a Microsoft shop,
we also planted ran-sition the AI agent

This allows users to access system,
the system from any application where co-pilot is available.
We'll also move the MCP server to a local machine

Finally, the system can generate reports automatically.
Either as Excel spreadsheets or directly in ACC,
making the results visible to the project team.
This effectively turns the MCP server into a govern execution layer
between AI and our project systems.
And this next section will go into more detail of our MCP server.
I'm sure by now that everyone here knows what an MCP server is,
but for the sake of completeness,
MCP stands for Model Context Protocol.
An MCP server acts as a bridge between an AI agent
and the real systems that needs to interact with.
They can provide APIs, data, credentials, prompts,
and workflows.
You can think of it as a governed orchestration-like service.
It also allows an AI agent to safely discover,
reason about, and act on real systems
in a secure, structured and audible way.
The guest started, I followed the open source setup guide
from the GitHub repository,
linked in the corner of the slide,
and created the server using Python.
The key concept inside an MCP server is something called a tool.
In a nutshell, when you give your AI agent a prompt,
it will search through a set of tools
which are similar to functions or methods,
except those have to be called directly by name.
With the tool, the agent reads through the definitions
and reasons whether or not that tool should be used
to accomplish its task.
If the agent determines that the tool is relevant,
it figures out what arguments to provide
and executes the code associated with that tool.
For most of my tools,
I actually use my IDE's AI coding assistance to generate them.
I would simply prompt something like,
can you write me an MCP tool that uses the Autodesk ACC APIs
to do blank?
And it would create a for me, and I could refine it as needed.
Whenever I needed access to another API or new process,
I would create another tool.
Because of how modular MCP tools are,
and how easy they are to create,
development moved extremely quickly.
So quickly, in fact, that at one point,
I had to pause development,
so the rest of the team could catch up
with their processes and test documents.
Once you understand MCP tools,
you realize you're not building one big application anymore.
You're building a capability library
that the AI can reason over.
But whether you're vibe coding or writing the traditional way,
one of the most important things to pay attention to
is a tool definition,
which looks like a Python doc string.
This is a string with a three double quotes on each side
following the tool name.
It describes what your tool's purpose is,
what arguments it takes, and what results it will return.
Like I said in the last slide,
your AI agent reads this description
to determine whether the tool can help
it accomplish this task it was given.
If the tool looks relevant,
the agent will generate the arguments
and execute the code associated with it.
The agent may also call helper functions inside your script.
Because of this, the clarity of the definition is critical.
The AI is essentially reasoning over these descriptions
to decide what actions it can take.
You can also use the definitions
to guide how prompts you trigger the tool.
For example, I added separate tools
for the Autodesk issues API and the reviews API.
And at first when I'd ask my agent
if there were any open reviews,
it would only look for issues and RFIs.
Part of this problem turned out to be terminology.
Because in ACC, document reviews are actually referred
to as document review markups.
So I updated the tool definition,
cooks explicitly say that if a user asks for reviews,
the agent should check the document review markups first
before checking issues and RFIs.
And one trick that's helpful to actually
is to actually have an AI agent write the tool description.
Since AI understands how other AI systems interpret language,
they can often produce clearer definitions
that we might write ourselves.
When I do that, I give it clear instructions
about the purpose of my tool, ways to call the tool,
and any warnings or edge cases
that the agent should watch out for when using it.
To make this approach viable at scale,
we had to be very intentional about the requirement
set from the start.
First, the solution couldn't depend on a single software silo.
Our teams already work across a wide ecosystem of tools
and introducing another tightly coupled system
would only add friction instead of reducing it.
We also need a visibility at the hub level,
not just one project at a time.
The system had to be able to look across ACC
at an enterprise scale,
whether that's by market sector, building type,
or overall portfolio health.
At the same time, we couldn't sacrifice detail.
Any analysis or automation needed to run
against live current project data,
so the decisions are always based
on what's happening right now.
Another key requirement was centralization of access,
not centralization of data.
We didn't want to move or duplicate project data.
Instead, we needed a single place
where we could securely connect to multiple APIs,
reason over that data and take action,
while letting teams continue working
in the tools they already use.
From a user perspective,
the experience also had to remain lightweight.
Engineers, project managers, and QA teams
shouldn't need to learn new systems
or manage complicated setups
just a bit of fit for automation.
On the development side,
speed and flexibility were critical.
We needed something that was easy to iterate on,
simple to extend and adaptable as requirements evolved,
which they always do.
That meant our developers needed to be able to plug in
new APIs, work closer data sources,
without constantly refactoring the entire system.
We also knew we needed reliable access to project data
across ACC docs and bill.
Finally, we wanted the architecture to be future proof,
not tied to one vendor, one platform,
or one specific API structure.
That way, if new tools come online
or existing systems evolve,
we should be able to plug them into the architecture
without redesigning everything.
These requirements ultimately become the foundation
that guided every technical decision we made moving forward.
So this next section goes over a few ways
we implemented our MCP server.

So the server can continuously interact with ACC
without manual intervention.
Each of these APIs
exposes a different part of the ACC ecosystem,
allowing the MCP server to both retrieve

As you can see, we were able to leverage GraphQL,

the AI agent can dynamically generate the GraphQL queries
